justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance solved via Redis Lua script compare-and-set, clearly explained as the core invariant. (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with durability trade-off explicitly acknowledged ('Redis is not durable by default — a crash could lose in-flight bid windows'). (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed via Timeout Scheduler with sorted set polling and highest-rated courier selection. (4) Hot key/zone risk identified ('hot zone' risk with rate-limiting mitigation). (5) Trade-offs include durability vs latency (Redis vs PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE comparison).

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed Redis Lua script approach with clear explanation of why WATCH/MULTI/EXEC is inferior under contention
Clock skew risk identified and mitigated by using Redis server TIME command — matches rubric's expected risk
Comprehensive implementation phasing (core atomicity → real-time → resilience) shows practical deployment thinking
Correctly rejected database-first approach with specific latency numbers (5-10ms per lock acquisition)
Identified the dual-win risk where a courier could win two simultaneous bid windows

Hot key risk is framed as 'hot zone' (many bid windows targeting same couriers) rather than the Redis-specific hot key problem (single Redis shard handling all keys for a zone), though zone-based partitioning implicitly addresses this

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved with Redis SETNX, explicitly called out as the core primitive. (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with durability trade-off clearly acknowledged — Redis crash loses active bid windows, AOF persistence discussed as mitigation. (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed via sorted-set delay queue selecting highest-rated courier. (4) Hot key risk addressed indirectly — single Redis instance is justified at current scale (500 windows), and Redis Cluster is explicitly deferred as unnecessary, with the scalability path noted. The trade-offs section includes durability vs. latency (Redis crash vs. sub-millisecond ops, PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE rejected for 5-15ms latency).

observations.text: Redis SETNX for atomic acceptance is front-and-center with clear rationale for why database approaches are rejected (5-15ms latency, lock contention)
Durability vs. latency trade-off is explicit: Redis crash loses ~167 orders during failover, with retry safety net designed
Fallback auto-assignment is well-designed with sorted-set delay queue, leader election for poller, and 20s safety-net retry
Excellent risk analysis including mobile network latency, connection storms, bot prevention, and stale availability data
Implementation guidance is unusually thorough with concrete file paths, vertical slicing strategy, and explicit pitfalls

Hot key problem not explicitly called out by name — at scale, if one Redis shard handles a popular zone's bid windows, this becomes an issue. The design sidesteps it by using a single Redis instance, but doesn't discuss what happens when scaling beyond that

{
  "executive_summary": "A real-time courier bidding system built around three core mechanisms: (1) WebSocket fan-out for sub-second job broadcast to eligible couriers, (2) Redis atomic compare-and-swap for race-free single-winner acceptance, and (3) a delayed-execution fallback that auto-assigns to the highest-rated courier when the 15-second window expires with no taker. The scale target (500 concurrent bid windows × 30 couriers/zone ≈ 15,000 peak connections) is well within single-cluster ca

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved via Redis Lua script (equivalent to SET NX, actually stronger — atomic compare-and-swap in a single Lua script). (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with explicit durability trade-off (AOF fsync, crash risk acknowledged, alternatives like PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE rejected with latency reasoning). (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed as a dedicated Auto-Assignment Handler component with ranked courier iteration and manual dispatch escalation. (4) Hot key/zone risk identified ('Hot zone problem' in risks section). (5) Durability vs. latency trade-off is the first item in trade_offs with detailed alternatives_considered.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough atomic claim design — Redis Lua script is arguably better than raw SET NX since it bundles state check + transition + winner assignment atomically
Clock skew risk explicitly addressed with mitigation (use Redis TIME command, not app server clocks) — matches rubric expected behavior
Explicitly rejects database-first approach in trade_offs and pitfalls_to_avoid with clear latency reasoning
Timer expiry handled via polling sorted set rather than Redis keyspace notifications, with well-reasoned justification for reliability
Hot zone problem identified with practical mitigation (rate-limit offers per courier, max N active offers)
Single Redis node SPOF risk identified with Sentinel failover mitigation and bid window re-creation strategy

Hot key problem is framed as 'hot zone' (many orders targeting same couriers) rather than the Redis-level hot key problem (single shard handling disproportionate traffic), though the scale-out flexibility point addresses zone-based sharding
